result:
I want to write about the moment the page is still blank and the whole heavy sky of possibility is hanging just above your head like a low thundercloud. People assume the terror lives in having nothing to say; the deeper fright is in having too much. Every word you choose is suddenly a door slamming shut on a dozen other corridors you will now never walk. I sit with my fingers twitching above the keys and feel the gravitational pull of every story I will not live because I am about to pick this story, this one brittle filament, and follow it into the dark.  

I used to think the cure for this vertigo was a plan: outline the cathedral before you carve the first stone. But plans are only polite fictions told by the past to the future, and the future is a prankster who loves to tear the maps in half. The only honest outline is the faint chalk line your own breathing leaves in night air, and even that is smudged by morning. So I have started to believe the opposite of planlessness is not planfulness; it is attendance. You pull up a chair inside the moment before language has happened and you sit there without reaching for either sword or shield. You let the storm pass through the bones. You become the waiting itself, the way a kettle waits for water, the way a violin waits for the twitch of the bow that makes it realize it was never wood at all but a throat.  

When I do that—when I manage to stay stubbornly present—I discover that writing is not carpentry, it is archaeology. The sentences are already there, strata of fossilized breath under the soles of the day. You brush away the dust of polite conversation, the calcified etiquette, the shale of old humiliations, and there they are: the footprints of the thing that hunted you through the savanna of childhood, the tiny vertebrae of a laugh you shared with a stranger on a train in 1997, the petrified wish you once made on a star that turned out to be a satellite. You do not invent; you exhume. And the deeper you dig, the more you find yourself in the sediment of common ownership. There is no private past. My grandmother’s war is your grandmother’s war if I let you hear the shrapnel still lodged in her lullabies. The page is not a mirror; it is a sliding door between two kitchens where strangers are tasting the same salt.  

Yet even as I write this, the commercial world wants me to believe that attention is a currency I should spend frugally, that readers are consumers with the attention span of a gnat on Red Bull. I am urged to hook, retain, monetize, pivot, brand. I am told to build a newsletter funnel, to optimize my “author platform,” to choose a niche the way a tourist chooses a postcard: small, pretty, instantly recognizable. The algorithmic governors of discovery insist that I become a single note, tirelessly struck, until readers can identify me in the dark by the tremor of one string. Against that narrowing, the blank page looks like a revolutionary tract. To write into the unknown is to riot against the empire of genre. To refuse to be a brand is to graffiti the subway ads of personal marketing. Every time I allow the paragraph to wander where it itches, I am basically chaining myself to the railing in front of the machine.  

And still, the question rises like reflux: what is it for, this refusal, this wandering, this luxurious waste of keystrokes? If a poem falls in the forest and no platform monetizes it, does it make a sound? I go outside to escape the question and find the neighborhood cottonwood answering with a shrug of pale seeds that drift across the alley like a slow summer snow. None of them will take root, but the tree does not amend its production values; it releases them anyway, a quiet manifesto against utility. I stand there long enough to remember that art behaves like pollen: it sticks to passersby, it irritates, it fertilizes invisibly. The measure is not the single grain but the forest that eventually remembers.  

Back upstairs, the cursor blinks like a metronome for heartbeats. I think of Keats’s idea of negative capability, the capacity to remain in “uncertainties, Mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason.” He wrote that letter in 1817, and we still treat it like a secret map, as though two centuries haven’t tried to sand it down into a slogan. The real trick is harder: to practice negative capability while the phone on the desk is buzzing with previews of replies that haven’t arrived yet, each message a small referendum on your worth, each red dot a wolf-whistle from the abyss. To write inside the twenty-first century is to practice Zen archery while someone nearby is live-streaming the target exploding for clout.  

I keep thinking about bodies. Underneath each word is a pulse that once kept time in meat. When I type “incandescent,” I remember the night I bicycled home from the library and the streetlights on the wet pavement looked like melting lemons and my lungs were trying out for the school track team of the cosmos. The word is a storage unit for that forgotten wattage. Readers sometimes ask where ideas originate; they want an address, a URL, a recipe. They don’t realize that every syllable is a scar, a tattoo inked by experience, and the only way to share it is to let someone else press their thumbprint into the skin of the letters until they feel the heat that once kept an animal alive.  

The clock on the oven says it is now 2:47 a.m. The world has shut its bright storefronts; even the sirens have gone to bed. Outside, the moon is practicing its chalk circle on the blacktop. Eventually, the horizon will bruise, the coffee shops will reopen, the podcast of morning commute will start to download itself into the avenues. None of that yet. Right now, the only citizenship left is here, on this raft of pixels drifting toward whatever country dawn will claim. I type because rowing keeps me from noticing the sharks of retrospect. I type because the ocean is wide and no one survives it, but the splash is music, and the stars are willing to drum along for a little while.  

A thousand words is a silly fence, a corral erected by an assignment that hoped to domesticate the mustang of thought. But mustangs don’t count paces; they gallop until the horizon forgets them, then they keep running inside the heads of people who once saw dust clouds shaped like possibility. Somewhere beyond this paragraph is a reader who will close the tab and go make tea and, while the kettle mutters, suddenly remember the smell of a classroom in fourth grade—paste, milk cartons, the brittle worksheets—and in that memory will be a sentence I never wrote, a sentence they will now write on the steamed window with one finger, a sentence that will outlive us both. That is the conspiracy I signed up for: the long, invisible relay of unbranded moments, smuggled across decades, disguised as ordinary breath.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
